15 min read
3 hours ago

--

Every frontend application is driven by events.
A user clicks a button, types into a search box, scrolls through a page, resizes the browser window, or drags an element across the screen. Each of these interactions generates one or more browser events, giving our application an opportunity to respond.
Most of the time, responding to every event seems perfectly reasonable. If a user types a character, we update the search results. If they scroll, we update the progress indicator. If they resize the window, we recalculate the layout.
The implementation works. The feature behaves as expected.
As applications grow, however, a different question starts to matter.
Does the application really need to respond to every single event?
Consider a search input connected to an API. Typing the word frontend doesn’t produce one event it produces nine separate keyboard events. If each event immediately triggers a network request, the application performs nine requests while the user is still typing a single word. From a functional perspective, nothing is broken. From a performance perspective, we’re doing significantly more work than necessary.
The same pattern appears in many other places. Scroll events can fire dozens of times within a second. Mouse movement generates a continuous stream of events. Window resize events keep firing for as long as the user drags the browser edge. In each case, the browser is simply reporting what the user is doing. It isn’t responsible for deciding how often our application should react that responsibility belongs to us.
This is where many performance discussions begin.
When developers first encounter debouncing and throttling, they’re often introduced as two utility functions used to limit how frequently another function executes. While that description is technically correct, it misses the more important idea.
Debouncing and throttling are not just implementation techniques. They are strategies for controlling how an application responds to user interactions.
The goal isn’t to make JavaScript faster. The goal is to avoid unnecessary work while preserving a responsive user experience.
Once you start looking at browser events through that lens, the choice between debouncing and throttling becomes much less about memorizing definitions and much more about understanding user behavior.
Throughout this article, we’ll begin with the problems that arise from high-frequency browser events, explore why they happen, implement both techniques from scratch, and discuss where each approach fits into modern frontend applications. By the end, the objective isn’t simply to know how debounce or throttle works it’s to understand the reasoning behind when and why each should be used.
Why Browser Events Need Control
Before looking at debouncing or throttling, it’s worth understanding the environment they’re designed for.
Modern browsers are highly event-driven. Almost everything a user does — typing, clicking, scrolling, resizing the window, dragging an element, or moving the mouse results in events being dispatched to the application. Event listeners give us a way to respond to those interactions, allowing the UI to stay dynamic and interactive.
The important detail is that browsers don’t evaluate whether an event is “necessary” for your application. Their responsibility is to accurately report user interactions as they happen.
If a user presses five keys, the browser dispatches five keyboard events.
If they scroll continuously for three seconds, the browser keeps dispatching scroll events throughout that interaction.
If they resize the browser window, resize events continue to fire until the user stops dragging.
From the browser’s perspective, this behavior is correct. It provides a complete stream of events and leaves the application to decide how to handle them.
The challenge begins when every event triggers expensive work.
“Expensive” doesn’t always mean a slow algorithm or a large computation. In frontend applications, it can be something as common as:

• Making an API request

• Filtering a large dataset

• Updating component state

• Recalculating layouts

• Triggering animations

• Rendering complex UI elements
Individually, none of these operations may seem significant. However, executing them repeatedly within a very short period can quickly become unnecessary work.
Consider a search input connected to a backend service.
A user types the word frontend in about a second.
The browser generates a sequence of keyboard events, one for each keystroke.
If every event immediately triggers a network request, the application ends up sending multiple requests even though the user hasn’t finished expressing their intent. In many cases, only the final value of the input is actually meaningful.
Now think about a different scenario.
Imagine a page displaying a reading progress indicator while the user scrolls through an article. Here, waiting until scrolling has completely finished would produce a poor user experience because the progress indicator would update only once. Instead, the application should continue responding during the interaction but not necessarily for every single scroll event.
These two examples highlight an important observation.
Not all browser events deserve the same response strategy.
Sometimes the application should wait until the interaction is complete.
Sometimes it should continue responding, but at a controlled frequency.
Recognizing this distinction is the first step toward building responsive and efficient frontend applications. Debouncing and throttling exist because different user interactions have different expectations, and treating every event identically often leads to unnecessary work or a degraded user experience.
A Common Frontend Problem: Search Isn’t as Simple as It Looks
A search input is one of the most common UI components in modern web applications. Whether you’re searching products in an e-commerce platform, filtering employees in an internal dashboard, or looking up repositories on a developer portal, the implementation usually starts with a straightforward idea.
Capture the user’s input, send it to the backend, and display the matching results.
A simplified implementation often looks something like this:
const handleSearch = async (event) => {
const query = event.target.value; const response = await fetch(`/api/search?q=${query}`);
const results = await response.json();
setResults(results);
};
At first glance, there’s nothing obviously wrong with this approach.
The code is easy to understand, the feature works, and the search results update as the user types.
The problem isn’t correctness.
The problem is frequency.
Every time the user presses a key, the input event fires, handleSearch executes, and a new network request is sent.
Imagine a user typing the word frontend.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every keystroke generates a new request. Most of these requests become obsolete before the user finishes typing.Now imagine this behavior across hundreds or thousands of users.
Even if your backend handles the load comfortably, the application is still performing work that adds little value. Most of those requests are based on incomplete input and are likely to be discarded by the next keystroke.
There’s another issue that’s easy to overlook.
Network requests don’t always complete in the same order they were sent.
Suppose the request for “fro” takes longer than the request for “front” because of network latency. Without additional handling, the slower response could arrive later and overwrite the newer search results with outdated data.
This is commonly referred to as a race condition, where multiple asynchronous operations compete to update the same piece of state.
So the search feature now has more than one concern:

• It sends more requests than necessary.

• It increases load on the backend.

• It performs unnecessary work in the browser.

• It can display stale data if responses arrive out of order.

• It may create a less responsive experience on slower networks.
At this point, it’s worth asking a different question.
Do we actually need to perform a search after every keystroke?
In many applications, the answer is no.
What we usually care about is the user’s final intent. If they’re still typing, there’s a good chance the current input is incomplete and will change again in a fraction of a second.
Instead of reacting immediately to every event, it would be more efficient to wait briefly and perform the search only after the user pauses typing.
That idea is the foundation of debouncing.
Before looking at its implementation, it’s helpful to understand the behavior we’re trying to achieve.
Understanding Debouncing: Waiting for the Right Moment
The search example from the previous section exposed an important observation.
The application wasn’t doing the wrong work — it was doing the right work too often.
What we actually wanted wasn’t to perform a search after every keystroke. We wanted to perform the search after the user had finished typing.
That small change in behavior is the idea behind debouncing.
Rather than executing a function immediately, debouncing waits for a short period of inactivity before allowing it to run. If another event occurs during that waiting period, the timer is reset and the waiting starts again.
In other words, every new interaction postpones the execution until the user finally pauses.
This behavior is easier to understand with a timeline.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The timer resets after every keystroke. The function executes only after the user stops interacting for the specified delay.Instead of sending multiple requests while the user is still typing, the application performs a single request using the final input.
The same idea applies beyond search inputs.
Whenever intermediate events provide little value and only the final interaction matters, debouncing can help reduce unnecessary work without affecting the overall user experience.
Implementing Debounce
Now that we’ve established the behavior we’re trying to achieve, the implementation becomes much easier to understand.
At its core, debouncing follows a simple sequence:

• Schedule the function to execute after a specified delay.

• If another event occurs before the delay expires, cancel the previously scheduled execution.

• Start a new timer.

• Execute the function only after the user has stopped interacting for the specified duration.
A reusable implementation looks like this:
function debounce(fn, delay) {
let timeoutId;
return function (...args) {
clearTimeout(timeoutId);
timeoutId = setTimeout(() => {
fn.apply(this, args);
}, delay);
};
}Although the implementation is only a few lines long, each part has a specific responsibility.
Storing the Timer
let timeoutId;The timer identifier is declared outside the returned function so that it can be reused across multiple invocations.
Each time the debounced function is called, it needs access to the timer created by the previous invocation. JavaScript preserves this variable through a closure, allowing every invocation to reference and update the same timer.
Cancelling the Previous Timer
clearTimeout(timeoutId);Every new interaction makes the previously scheduled execution obsolete.
Calling clearTimeout prevents the earlier timer from completing, ensuring that only the most recent interaction remains scheduled for execution.
Without this step, every invocation would create an independent timer, and the original problem of multiple executions would still exist.
Scheduling the Next Execution
timeoutId = setTimeout(() => {
fn.apply(this, args);
}, delay);After cancelling the previous timer, a new one is scheduled.
If another event occurs before the delay has elapsed, this timer is cancelled as well and replaced with a new one. This process continues until the user pauses long enough for the timer to complete.
Only then is the or
